md: **Area** is the amount of 2D space inside a closed shape, measured in *square units*. Each common shape has its own formula. Memorize the formulas, but understand *why* they work — the 'why' often comes from rearranging the shape into a rectangle, where area is just length × width.
These are the foundations: area = length × width.
Same as a rectangle! The trick: cut a triangle off one end of the parallelogram and slide it to the other end. You get a rectangle with the same base and height. So the area is the same.

blocks.title: Watch the height
blocks.body: The height in $bh$ is **perpendicular to the base** — not the slanted side. For a parallelogram or triangle, you measure the height *straight up* from the base, not along the side.

md: Why the half? Take any triangle and copy it, rotated 180°. The two together form a parallelogram with the same base and height. So one triangle has half the parallelogram's area.
Think of it as the average of the two bases times the height — the trapezoid's area equals a *rectangle's area* with the same height and width equal to the average of the two bases.
(See the Circle Vocabulary subtopic for why $\pi$ shows up.)

blocks.title: Quick reference
blocks.body: **Square**: $s^2$. **Rectangle**: $lw$. **Parallelogram**: $bh$. **Triangle**: $\frac{1}{2}bh$. **Trapezoid**: $\frac{1}{2}(b_1 + b_2)h$. **Circle**: $\pi r^2$. Always use the *perpendicular* height (not slant).

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: Six formulas for the most common shapes. Almost all derive from the rectangle's $l \cdot w$ — by rearranging or pairing. The key skill is identifying which length is the perpendicular **height**, especially for parallelograms, triangles, and trapezoids.
